data.title: PennantPark Investment: Dividend Cut May Not Happen After All (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

PennantPark Investment (NYSE:PNNT) operates as a business development company that aims to generate attractive total returns through its portfolio of diverse debt investments. PNNT is a bit more unique compared to other BDCs because of its larger focus on equity-based investments, which may not produce consistent cash flows and show a greater sensitivity to interest rate changes. I previously covered PNNT and issued a sell rating due to a weaker portfolio strength proven by growing non-accruals, an unjustified higher valuation, and weakening distribution coverage. Now that we've crossed into the new year and PNNT reported their Q1 earnings, I wanted to provide an updated look into its portfolio and valuation based on the shifted outlook.

Looking at the performance over the last 12 months, PNNT's price has increased by 7.6%. When including distributions, the total return jumps up to 22.7% over the same period. One of the main appeals of PNNT is that it offers a high dividend yield of about 13.3%. This double-digit yield makes it very attractive for investors seeking a high level of income generation within their portfolio. However, I don't believe that PNNT's dividend is the safest within the BDC sector, as the base net investment income doesn't cover the distribution. As it stands, management is using cash from undistributed spillover to make up the difference in the short term. Assuming earnings stay the same, management can stretch this capital out for two years.

In terms of valuation and price stability, I will admit that PNNT has held up a lot better than expected. The price run has caused the discount to NAV level to shrink considerably. However, I don't necessarily see any indication of strong growth that supports this shrinking discount. Non-accruals at cost haven't shown much improvement and earnings continue to trend downward. While there are some positives in terms of portfolio growth related to PNNT's Joint Venture, I am not fully convinced yet. So let's start by taking a look at PNNT's portfolio strategy.

Portfolio Strategy

PNNT's portfolio currently has a total value of approximately $1.4B, spanning across 158 direct investments. Although the latest quarterly update was reported in February, management releases their portfolio data on a calendar year breakdown, so the latest information is from December 2024. PNNT focuses on making investments into companies that have an EBITDA between $10M to $50M. From a sector focus, PNNT is primarily concentrated between the following investment areas:

  • Healthcare
  • Government Services
  • Software & Technology
  • Consumer
  • Business Services

More importantly, the range of investments is pretty diverse, with an average weight of 0.6% per position across its entire portfolio. First lien senior secured investments make up 49% of the portfolio, which is a decrease from the 58% weight in first lien investments at the start of 2024. This is notable because first lien senior secured investments sit at the top of the corporate capital structure, meaning they have the highest priority for repayment. Debt investments structured this way are generally considered to be more defensive, since it can protect a BDC from losing all invested capital in a bad transaction.

If an underlying borrower is underperforming and defaults on their loan, they may start to liquidate assets in order to pay down their obligations. In this case, PNNT would be entitled for repayment first since its capital was structured as first lien debt. This increases the probability for PNNT to get some of their capital back. In addition, PNNT has increased their weight in equity-based investments up to 23%. Equity investments don't produce consistent levels of income, but they have the potential to provide a greater amount of capital appreciation if the underlying borrower's valuation increases. Lastly, PNNT's Joint Venture accounts for 6% of the portfolio, but there are signs that this will become a much larger part of the portfolio at some point. When referring to the Joint Venture, management is specifically talking about PennantPark Senior Loan Fund (PSLF).

Additionally, the majority of PNNT's portfolio operates on a floating rate basis. In theory, this should've allowed PNNT to benefit from a higher interest income. A higher interest rate environment directly correlates to a higher interest expense that borrowers have to pay if their debt is structured on a floating rate basis. The downside is that a higher interest rate puts pressure on any portfolio companies that are underperforming, and this can lead to an increased rate of defaults/non-accruals.

Q1 Earnings

PNNT recently reported their Q1 earnings and the result were mixed. Most notably, net investment income landed at $0.20 per share, which was a 10% decrease from the prior quarter's net investment income of $0.22 per share. Additionally, We can see that the fair value of their direct investments have slightly decreased quarter over quarter. However, this was offset by growth in both the Joint Venture Investments and Cash on hand, amounting to $208M and $239M, respectively.

The Joint Venture portion of PNNT's portfolio is becoming a more meaningful piece of the cash flow as management puts emphasis on its growth. For instance, the Joint Venture portfolio is estimated to grow to $1.6B over the year, which can improve PNNT's earnings. The Joint Venture has contributed 18.4% return on invested capital over the last twelve months. From what I can see, this Joint Venture will be the tool that management uses to reduce the overall exposure to equity investments, as they aim to rotate existing equity investments into high-yielding loan assets over 2025.

Additionally, management has allocated $296M towards new investments for the quarter. This was a significant improvement over prior quarters. This was allocated towards 12 new portfolio companies and 61 existing portfolio companies, at a weighted average yield of 10.6%. However, this was likely due to the fact that sales and repayments amounted to $354M, leading to a net decrease of $58M in investment activity for the quarter.

I would like to see an improvement in the net growth in future quarters because there weren't really any quarters where PNNT delivered impactful growth through new investments. An improvement here would ultimately lead to better net investment income performance. The muted net investment income performance resulted in the lack of meaningful NAV growth over the last two years. While a steady NAV is okay, we can argue that the current interest rate environment should have resulted in better results from an increased net investment income.

  • Mar 2023 NAV: $7.60
  • June 2023 NAV: $7.72
  • Sept 2023 NAV: $7.70
  • Dec 2023 NAV: $7.65
  • Mar 2024 NAV: $7.69
  • June 2024 NAV: $7.52
  • Sept 2024 NAV: $7.56
  • Dec 2024 NAV: $7.57

Updated Valuation & Risk Profile

Since PNNT is a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. AS a result, PNNT's price currently trades at a slight discount to NAV of 5%. Looking over the last decade, the current discount level is actually a lot higher than PNNT's usual range. The price frequently traded at a discount to NAV greater than 20% over the last decade. Just for reference, the price traded at an average discount to NAV of 19% over the last three-year period. I have a bit of trouble determining what justifies the shrinking discount level.

For instance, NAV growth is almost non-existent and net investment income levels haven't risen. While PNNT's total assets have steadily increased, I do not think this warrants the discount level to steadily shrink the way that it has. For instance, non-accruals haven't really improved since the time of my last coverage. When it comes to measuring the risk profile of a BDC, I like to monitor changes in the non-accrual rate over time. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming their original expectations and aren't actively contributing towards a BDC's net investment income levels anymore.

At the time of my last coverage, non-accruals amounted to 4.2% of cost and 2.5% of fair portfolio value. As of the latest earnings call, management confirmed that the non-accrual rate now sits at 4.3% of cost and 1.5% of market value. The non-accruals only amount to 2 different portfolio companies, with the most recent addition being Pragmatic Institute. Management sees some restructuring to take place with the borrowers in non-accrual status.

Our current assumption is that during this quarter, the quarter ended March, there will be some form of restructuring there, some debt will be converted and there'll be a yield instrument coming out of that. - Art Penn, CEO

While non-accruals aren't the entire picture, they definitely have an influence on the future success of a BDC. For me personally, the non-accrual rate can also be an indication of how strong management's underwriting capabilities are when assessing a potential investment. As interest rates remain high, I do not feel confident that the non-accrual rate will significantly improve over 2025, especially since the outlook has shifted. The Fed's original expectation was that there would be roughly four interest rate cuts to happen.

Since inflation has remained stubborn, the Fed has shifted to a more hawkish stance and now only estimate a single interest rate cut for the year. Higher rates will continue to put pressure on the portfolio. Just for a point of reference, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs:

  • Capital Southwest (CSWC): 2.7% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): 0.4% non-accrual rate at fair value.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.3%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by 3 to get a quarterly conversion, we land at a quarterly payout amount of $0.24 per share. As previously mentioned, net investment income landed at $0.20 per share for the quarter, which still indicates that its base distribution is not fully covered by net investment income. However, it seems like management is comfortable with this because of the excess of distributable spillover of $0.99 per share.

For the quarter ended December 31st, our GAAP and core net investment income was $0.20 per share, which is $0.04 below our quarterly dividend. PNNT has $65 million, or $0.99 per share of undistributed spillover income. In order to continue to comply with the tax rules, we are required to distribute the spillover income over time. We believe PNNT can generate core NII of $0.21 to $0.22 per share. However, if core NII remained at the current level of $0.20 per share, it would take over 24 quarters or six years to fully distribute the spillover income. - Art Penn, CEO

Management essentially has to use this spillover to fund the dividend. So by this metric, the dividend probably won't be cut in the short term as long as there is excess spillover that needs to be distributed. However, I would like to see management turn some of this spillover into cash by growing their baseline of investments that generate income. In the short term, there's even a possibility that PNNT implements some sort of larger supplemental in order to pay out this spillover balance. It seems to be up in the air right now, but I wouldn't anticipate anything like this happening until later in the year, when management more clearly lays out their investment plans in future quarters.

It's worth mentioning that the distributions received from PNNT aren't the most tax efficient, since they are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends are usually taxed as ordinary income, which isn't ideal when compared to the qualified dividends you'd receive from traditional dividend paying stocks. Based on your specific income needs and amount of capital invested, it may be best to utilize PNNT within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating back to a hold, as the dividend coverage isn't as questionable as it once seemed. PNNT holds two years' worth of undistributed spillover income that it will be using to make up the difference in net investment income. Therefore, the current distribution may be safe in the short term, but this still doesn't take away from the fact that net investment income isn't supporting the base distribution anymore. This takes away from confidence when we consider that the rate of investment activity hasn't been large enough to lead to any meaningful growth in earnings. Additionally, I believe that the valuation is a bit expensive at the moment as non-accruals haven't seen any significant improvements. For now, I plan to stay on the sidelines and continue to monitor changes over the next few quarters.

data.title: Regency Centers: A Strong Business With A Weak Investment Case
data.description:
Jacksonville, Florida-based real estate investment trust ("REIT"), Regency Centers Corporation (NASDAQ:REG), is known as an owner-operator and developer of high-quality grocery-anchored shopping centers that are strategically positioned in affluent suburban trade areas across the country. The company has a long history of operational excellence, possesses a strong business model, many competitive advantages, robust financials, and disciplined investment policies. Nevertheless, the company is grossly overpriced, making it unattractive as an investment, even as it is attractive as a business.

The Business Model

Regency Centers benefits from the long-run fundamentals underpinning neighbourhood and community shopping centers, with its focus on grocery-anchored retail properties. As of the end of 2023, Regency Centers had full or partial ownership interests in 482 properties whose crown jewels are market-leading grocery stores, covering some 56.8 million square feet of gross leasable area ("GLA").

Regency Centers earns around 97% of its revenue from long-term leases with national and regional retailers, with a great focus on necessity-based tenants such as grocery stores, pharmacies, restaurants, and service-oriented businesses.

Regency Centers’ grocery stores and essential retail businesses offering non-discretionary goods and services, ensure consistent foot traffic and tenant stability. Regency Centers manages its portfolio of properties through acquisitions, dispositions, and redevelopment projects, tilting toward high-growth suburban markets with attractive demographic trends. Regency Centers enjoys strong leasing activity, executing more than 2,032 new and renewal leases totalling over 9.9 million Pro-rata square feet ("SF"), with a blended rent spread of 9.5% in 2024, compared to 1,839 new and renewal leases representing 6.9 million Pro-rata SF with a rent spread of 10% in 2023. Regency Centers has a disciplined, prudent approach to new developments and redevelopments, targeting high-yield projects that enhance long-term value.

Strong Financial Performance

Pro-rata same property net operating income ("NOI"), excluding termination fees/expenses, grew 3.1% year-over-year, while the occupancy rate reached a record-high 96.7% leased rate as of the end of 2024, with shop space occupancy reaching 94.1% in that time. Regency Centers’ funds from operations ("FFO") rose from $4.15 per share in 2023 to $4.30 per share in 2024. Core operating earnings rose 5% year-over-year, highlighting the REIT’s solid operational execution.

For comparability across industries, I like to convert earnings to net operating profit after tax ("NOPAT"), and this is revealing here because Regency Centers’ NOPAT is growing much faster than its reported earnings, with NOPAT rising from $248.6 million in 2020 to $547.3 million in 2024, compounding at a rate of 17.1% a year.

The company has used its growing profitability to invest $258 million in new projects that commenced in 2024, which have strengthened Regency Centers’ competitive positioning. In addition, it has compounded dividends by 3.8% a year since 2014, with the most recent increase raising the dividend to $0.705 per share per quarter.

Regency Centers Has Durable Competitive Advantages

Regency Centers have a number of durable competitive advantages. Firstly, its high-quality portfolio of assets anchored largely in grocery centers in affluent suburban markets, reduces exposure to volatility in consumer spending. Secondly, Regency Centers has developed strong relationships with its tenants, such as Whole Foods, Publix, and The Kroger Co. (KR), driving long-term lease stability and rental income. The company’s experienced management team gives it an edge in terms of execution, and navigating economic cycles while attaining growth and profitability. For tenants looking to work with sustainable partners, Regency Centers has been recognised as an industry leader in Environmental, Social and Governance ("ESG") initiatives, enhancing its long-run value proposition.

Disciplined Capital Allocation

Regency Centers manages its capital conservatively, with a low leverage ratio, investment-grade credit ratings (BBB+/Baa1), and over $1 billion in available liquidity. The company aims for a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of around 5.2x, giving it financial flexibility and resilience. This puts it in good stead for downturns in the market. Regency Centers development and redevelopments are expected to receive $1 billion in capital over the next five years. That it's so careful with its capital is even more important because it means that it will not borrow at unfavorable terms or make deals that will hurt shareholder value. In terms of its acquisitions and dispositions, management is careful to only procure high-quality centers while divesting its non-core assets to increase long-term value. Regency Centers’ return on invested capital ("ROIC") has improved over time from 2.5% in 2020 to 4.8% in 2024, showing the success of management’s capital allocation, although, those ROIC improvements are slowing.

Valuation

We can calculate Regency Centers’ intrinsic value by capitalizing its current NOPAT by its cost of capital of 7.24%, and then adding its net deferred tax liability of $3.83 million, its $4.4 billion in total debt, and deducing its $225 million in preferred capital, and $176 million in minority interest. This gives us an intrinsic value of $2.75 billion, or $15.11 per share, compared to an intrinsic value of $73.44 per share. With a market cap so far in excess of its intrinsic value, Regency Centers, despite its strong economics, is overvalued.

Conclusion

Despite strong economics, prudent capital allocation, consistent dividend growth and an attractive portfolio of assets, Regency Centers is not an attractive investment opportunity given its current overvaluation. Investors are advised to put the business on a watchlist, rather than immediately investing.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: Another Rally To Sell
data.description:
D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS) is a promising quantum computing startup with a suddenly hot stock. The company has intriguing annealing quantum technology and systems running production tasks already, but another tech giant has jumped into the space with impressive quantum silicon. My investment thesis is Bearish on D-Wave Quantum after the stock has soared the last few months while the prospects of the business haven't exactly improved at a corresponding rate.

Limited Business

D-Wave enters 2025 with multiple tech giants releasing impressive quantum computing chips, while the company only forecast 2024 bookings in the range of $23 million. The quantum computing company did obtain a system order from Forschungszentrum Jülich and recently placed a system into production with NTT Docomo in Japan.

The new D-Wave processor includes 4,440 qubits and the company has the world's largest quantum computer running a cloud service with SLAs. D-Wave makes a lot of claims regarding leading quantum production systems and services, but the business didn't even report revenue growth in the last reported quarter.

Microsoft (MSFT) just announced a new quantum chip called the Majorana 1 with a new material, which is not a solid, liquid or gas. The topological material apparently provides the quantum chip with the path to 1 million qubits, which is the holy grail of quantum computing.

D-Wave appears to have a lot of competition of quantum computing startups and tech giants. D-Wave saw Q3 bookings dip to only $2.3 million, with just $5.6 million through the first 9 months of the year.

D-Wave will report Q4 results later in March, where the market expects strong bookings at $18 million after the company provided preliminary numbers back in January. The December quarter bookings are set to soar 500% from the minimal prior-year bookings of $3 million, but the annual bookings will only jump 120% in 2024.

A big key to the earnings report will be the staying power of the bookings total, or whether the majority were one off from the system sale. The limited bookings in the prior quarters suggest the numbers are more one off.

The key QCaaS revenues were up 41% in Q3, but the amount was only $1.6 million. Back on the Q324 earnings call, the company confirmed the NTT Docomo deal would amount to around $1 million QCaaS revenues in both a positive sign and an indication of the long road ahead to reach material revenues.

Not A Great Risk/Reward

The company started Q4 by paying off a term loan and ending up with only $30 million in cash, but the company has raised considerable amounts of cash the last couple of months. D-Wave raised $175 million in ATM offerings during Q4 with the latest $75 million offering at an average price of $4.81.

The company recently raised another $150 million via selling 25 million shares in the ATM equity offering program at an average price of $6.10 per share. In theory, D-Wave now has somewhere around $325 million in cash to provide a nice war chest to complete in the quantum computing race.

D-Wave enters 2025 with a far better cash position, but the quantum computing company has quickly rushed out to raise cash on every rally. Management thought the company was getting great value, buying back in the $4s.

The company reported an adjusted EBITDA loss of $13.8 million in Q3, with a target for a $54.3 million loss for the year. D-Wave isn't losing a lot of money here, but the company likely raised cash to boost spending.

Despite the production system deals, D-Wave is still years away from meaningful revenues. The consensus analyst estimates are for revenues to reach $16 million in 2025 and still only $34 million in 2026.

The stock has a listed market cap of $1.9 billion after another rally despite the limited bookings and revenues following the additional excitement in the space with the Microsoft news. Quantum computing definitely has the potential for explosive growth once adopted in mass, but D-Wave faces far too much competition to assume this small player is a sector winner.

The CEO made major headlines refuting NVIDIA (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang on the timeline for quantum computing to become useful. Alan Baratz might be correct that D-Wave has annealing quantum computing products now, but the business isn't material.

Investors need material revenues to improve the risk/reward equation at the current bookings levels, and consensus forecasts suggest a big issue here. With tech giants like Microsoft already innovating in the sector, D-Wave isn't scaling fast enough to out-muscle the tech giants.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that D-Wave Quantum is an interesting quantum computing player to watch. Though, the stock doesn't have a lot of appeal, as all of the promising business in the works doesn't even add up to material revenues.

Investors should use the recent rally to cash out of D-Wave at nearly 130x sales estimates for 2026. The company could have a huge future in quantum computing, but D-Wave could just as easily struggle to compete with the tech giants already innovating the space, leading to the stock being worth a lot less in the years ahead.

data.title: On Holding: Building Up My Thesis, Averaging Up On The Stock
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Being in growth mode, On Holding (NYSE:ONON) isn’t yet optimized for free cash flow generation, yet it has expanded its FCF margin to an incredible 18.8% over the TTM period. As ONON continues to expand internationally, build out its new retail stores, grow apparel sales, and increase DTC channel penetration, it has an opportunity to expand margins even further.

This is where my opinion on the stock and valuation differs from analysts who have cited decelerating top-line growth as reasons to downgrade the stock. I see FCF margin expansion and sustained top-line growth potentially powering ONON past its all-time highs in the months or years ahead.

Gross margins remain robust, sitting at 60.2%, and OCF margin has dramatically expanded to 21.5%. Operating margin is near its all-time high, currently at 9.2%, despite some logistical challenges and expansion costs that impacted operating profits during Q2 and Q3. The recent sell-off in ONON stock represents a buying opportunity that I plan to take advantage of.

Looking at the TTM financial picture, ONON has shown steady revenue growth, steady gross margin expansion, and an inflection in operating and operating cash flow margins.

Introduction and Business Update

In June of last year, I wrote about how On has the foundations for success in the running and performance shoe category and as a winning investment, in my article, On Holding’s Long Runway For Growth Looks Intriguing.

First, has innovative, differentiated, and appealing shoe technology and design. I was so impressed that I bought two pairs, one in the store and one through the DTC channel. Second, I learned that On’s shoes are flying off the shelves in my local sports basement store, rarely, if ever, landing on the clearance rack. The shoes aren’t everyone’s taste, but they are wildly popular. Third, On isn't even close to being fully represented in the shelf space of major retailers, giving it plenty of room to grow in the years ahead.

I also highlighted ON’s operating leverage, expecting the company to be able to expand margins, particularly FCF margin. My base DCF case didn't even factor in and expansion of FCF margin.

I will use the chart below to outline what has happened to the stock since my first article and then explain why I think the price action of late is unwarranted for long-term investors.

Shortly after my article, the company performed better than expected Q1 results, and my expectations were exceeded. I was lucky enough to have gotten into the stock before the resetting of market expectations due its free cash flow inflection. I rode the stock price up.

For Q2, the company reported a CHF 93.3 million improvement in FCF, bringing TTM FCF from a loss of CHF 154 million to profits of CHF 365 million. The first green arrow shows the date of Q2 earnings. The stock performed well prior tot Q3 earnings.

In Q3, On backed up its strong Q2 performance with a 40.6% increase in FCF YoY to CHF 140.5 million. This brought the company's TTM free cash flow to CHF 405.4 million versus a mark of CHF -2.3 million in the prior TTM period (Q4 2022 – Q3 2023). By this time, ONON's cash-generating potential was clear to see. The inflection was so dramatic that it took me by surprise. Green arrow #2 shows the date of Q3 earnings. The strength of On’s FCF in Q3 and management’s guidance towards the high end of its previous EBITDA margin guide, with the potential to even better its guidance, sent the stock even higher.

One point of concern from analysts was ONON’s decelerating revenue growth rates. TTM revenue was only 26.1% higher in Q3, continuing a deceleration of the top-line growth rate. I believe some investors might be missing the mark here, prompting some negative analyst opinions on Seeking Alpha. 26.1% revenue growth is still strong enough for ONON to attain its 2023 investor day target of doubling net sales from 2023 to 2026. Despite the deceleration, there was more than enough operating leverage for the company to generate higher cash flows. These fears may have contributed to the recent sell-off.

When Deckers Outdoor (DECK) reported earnings on 1/31/25, providing softer than expected guidance, the entire shoe manufacturing market was a little shook. Since then (indicated on the chart by a red arrow), ONON has sold off by 23.9% (as of 2/22/25). I think the market may be either reducing risk and taking gains or just simply shifting into safer assets, which could be explained by the consumer staples stocks having a huge week last week. The market may also be waiting to see if ONON will report any reason to doubt its 2025 targets, and decelerating growth rates might have led to these doubts.

Despite decelerating top-line growth, the company's profitability continues to shine through, and I believe the runway for growth remains strong for On. Despite the higher valuation and stock price, I maintain a buy rating on the company.

One of my focuses in my November article on ONON was its LightSpray technology and the potential for this revolutionary idea to drive low-cost production advantages and greatly expand the company’s ability to drive sales at lower costs. However, that initiative is many years away from becoming a major part of the business, and some uncertainty exists as to how successful it will be and how much adoption that technology will gain.

In this article, I will be focusing on the more near and medium-term growth drivers for ONON. While revenue growth is a requirement, I am focusing on revenue growth in three key areas of opportunity for ONON. Those areas are international growth, particularly in the Asia-Pacific region ("APAC"), apparel sales growth, and direct-to-consumer ("DTC") growth.

Key Growth Drivers

International Growth

One key to On’s success will be continuing growth outside of its core markets of the USA and Europe. The company has been posting incredible growth in the APAC region lately, with YoY quarterly growth expanding from 58.1% in Q4 of 2023 to 79.3% in Q3 of 2024, on a reported basis. It plans to invest in this region by growing retail doors to expand brand awareness.

The company opened several stores in China, including a larger flagship format in premium locations, and is benefitting from high e-commerce growth in the market. They plan to open at least two more in China during 2025. ONON also plans to open a second premium location in Tokyo ahead of the World Athletics Championships (Track and Field) in 2025. I believe that a rabid fan base that unfortunately missed out on the opportunity to witness the 2021 Olympic Games in person will be enthused about the championships, and On will be able to expand its brand awareness, similar to the success it had during the Paris Olympic Games in 2024. In Korea, the company will be entering its first full year with a distribution facility in the country. Additionally, On has the Philippines and Indonesia on its radar for expansions.

Apparel Growth

On continues to grow its apparel business rapidly, but the base on which it grows is still minuscule, representing only 4.0% of total TTM sales. One of the drivers of apparel sales is expected to be an increase in global retail door openings. On expects a total of 20 new retail door openings in 2024, and an additional 20 to 25 stores in 2025. The company has had success in increasing apparel sales penetration relative to its wholesale efforts through its retail locations, during the Q3 2023 earnings call, On mentioned that apparel made up 23% of sales in its Miami, FL location and 19% of sales in London.

During the most recent earnings call, Caspar Coppetti stated that On envisions that its retail stores can realistically drive a 25% or 30% apparel sales penetration share. This is another potential inflection point for the business that it has not been able to recognize yet, as it is fighting to gain apparel shelf space with its wholesale partners.

On can control what goes on the shelves and display apparel for its customers, allowing it to build the On apparel brand. Once ONON’s apparel is more visible, this could drive higher adoption through the wholesale channel as demand grows for major retailers to start stocking it.

The global athleisure apparel market represents a large and largely untapped opportunity by On. It was estimated at $384.0 billion in 2024, and is expected to grow to $740.7 billion by 2032, a CAGR of 8.6%. ONON only did CHF $86.9 million in apparel over the TTM period, equivalent to roughly $96.75 million USD.

DTC Growth

Growth in DTC has been impressive and a driver of greater margins for ON, the company grew its DTC channel by 49.8% (50.7% constant currency) in Q3, and expanded DTC to 38.8% of net sales. Over the TTM period, DTC has grown to CHF 853.2 million, a 38.8% increase YoY. While the growth rate has slowed, it looks like it may have formed a bottom since Q2.

On is working on optimizing its supply chain with a fully automated Atlanta distribution facility to support sales in the USA. The company has been lapping tough growth but is also coming off of a challenging period when distribution was impacted by problems in Q2, which caused supply bottlenecks.

Risks

Wholesale Growth Must Accelerate To Spark A Rally

ONON’s wholesale channel has been decelerating significantly, and even though it's lapping tough comps, I want to see more growth here. I think that the supply chain disruptions and lack of manufacturing capacity that ONON has dealt with may have finally caught up with them in Q3, as wholesale only grew 19% YoY. Any re-acceleration in this channel will likely lead to a re-acceleration in total sales.

If a sustained rally is to take place, I think the company either needs to outperform expectations in its DTC and international growth rates soon or wholesale needs to perform better.

Competition And Innovation Risk

Another risk comes in the form of intense competition for footwear and apparel, and the potential for On’s innovative ideas either falling flat or other competitors coming in with either better or comparable and cheaper products.

However, On is building its brand awareness, and if it goes from being more of a niche company to becoming a brand powerhouse, the stock could be a major winner for long-term investors.

Valuation

I have compiled a reverse DCF model to help determine what kind of free cash flow growth the market currently expects out of ONON’s share price. All figures are presented in USD for the purposes of the model.

Using ONON’s TTM FCF of $451.9 million USD, I have built a 20-year intrinsic value reverse DCF model to show how much growth and for how long ONON might have to achieve to justify the current stock price. The discount rate doubles as a stock price hurdle rate. When the current share price matches the estimated intrinsic value, the stock, in theory, could generate a 14.1% CAGR if ONON grows free cash flow as shown.

My projected required growth rate starts at a CAGR of 25% for years 1-5, 18% for years 6-10, 12% for years 11-15, 7% in years 16 and 17, and 5% in years 18-20. The declining growth rate over the 20-year period accounts for business maturity. Given ONON’s inflection point for free cash flow generation, the potential for a mid-twenties revenue growth rate for the next few years, and the company's clear operating leverage and premium pricing power, these growth rates appear possible, though not at all certain.

Valuation Model Risk

A 20-year model will be incorrect, even more so than a five-year model. However, it effectively illustrates the required growth needed for a younger company with a big market opportunity. Though I believe this to be a reasonable growth rate, if ONON is able to win in the areas I have discussed above, this would be quite a growth story.

Conclusion

The company continues to lay the foundation for the next phase of growth. If it can sustain this growth while building brand awareness, international sales, DTC growth and apparel penetration On should be able to see huge free cash flow gains in the years ahead.

Given that On hasn’t fully tapped into its market opportunity yet and is generating impressive margins, the growth story appears intact, and the stock looks like a relative value right now after falling from all-time highs. I rate the stock a buy and plan to add to my position.

I am placing my bets today on ONON in the shoe manufacturing space. The potential range of outcomes for On is much wider than for more mature companies, like Nike (NKE) and DECK, meaning the market has a greater opportunity to misunderstand or underestimate the opportunity.

Because I believe the opportunity isn’t fully understood, I rate ONON a buy and will continue adding to my position after the market has given me a gift of lower prices.

data.title: HYG Vs. LQD: Excess Returns With Lower Duration, Minor Credit Risk Increase
data.description:
The bond market is in a strange place amid the various fiscal and monetary policy changes that have occurred and that are likely to occur in the future. We recently covered a series of CLO instruments; however, we decided to dial in on corporate bond ETFs today by covering the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) and the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:HYG). While our current broad-based systematic thoughts are anti-risk, we identified a few interesting attributes within each of these vehicles upon assessing them.

Are you wondering which provides the best risk-return profile? Here's our take.

What Are They?

These are two exchange-traded funds that diversify security-specific risk by investing in a broad range of bonds, which, in turn, isolates systematic credit risk. The following diagram illustrates LQD ETF's methodology and historical returns, providing investors with a sense of its risk-return profile.

Here's a look at HYG ETF. The ETF has generated excess returns versus LQD since its inception. In isolation, you'd say that it would trivially generate excess returns in the long-run. However, as illustrated later, it's not necessarily the case.

Anything rated below BBB- is usually considered non-investment grade. As illustrated below, HYG emphasises BB, B, and CCC. However, note that its exposure can change with time. On the other end, LQD is primarily exposed to A and BBB-rated bonds, and like HYG, LQD's portfolio might shift in time.

I think an important takeaway here is that LQD isn't an investment grade conviction play, in other words, much of its exposure is to lower-rated investment grade bonds as opposed to elite investment grade bonds. Similarly, the bulk of HYG's exposure is borderline high-yield instead of ultra-speculative or distressed.

A time series shows that HYG has outperformed LQD in the past decade. However, notice that HYG and LQD's correlation often departs, suggesting differentiated influencing variables. Risk-return is examined later in the article. Nonetheless, HYG's long-return returns certainly provides a talking point.

Market Commentary

High-yield bonds usually outperform investment grade bonds when the economy is firm. Although we see an economic peak emerging at some stage, the U.S.'s real economic growth arguably remains robust and recent re-inflation conveys consumer confidence. We believe residual GDP growth might persist, largely driven by technological innovation, which is contributing to Total Factor Productivity. Sure, we could see a slowdown in economic growth through Donald Trump's trade war and inward economic policies. However, unlike many other economies, we are bullish about the U.S. economy for 2025, even after considering its isolated headwinds.

Here's an overview of interest rates, credit spreads, and CPI to set a base for a discussion that follows beneath.

HYG's weighted average maturity is 3.77 years versus LQD's 12.96 years. This naturally lowers HYG's effective duration to 3.05, substantially lower than LQD's 8.18x. What does this mean? HYG gains approximately 3.05% for a 1% decrease in interest rates and vice versa; the same relationship holds true for LQD, to a larger extent. Considering our view of GDP and inflation, we think lower duration bonds are better placed in today's market, given the higher-for-longer interest rate environment. Sure, higher rates can increase underlying bond coupons; however, we are arguing from a relative standpoint here.

Furthermore, LQD has a positive convexity of 1.16 whereas HYG has a negative convexity. The positive convexity softens LQD's downside risk and amplifies its upside price gains; the opposite holds true for HYG, which has negative convexity. Despite convexity's appearance, it's a secondary effect and likely negligible relative to a duration and spread effect.

Speaking of spreads, HYG's option-adjusted spread is much wider than LQD's. Therefore, an increase in corporate credit spreads might devalue its portfolio. Both non-investment grade and investment grade spreads remain tight for the time being. However, they can be mean-reverting. Therefore, we highlight spreads as a potential risk factor. Sure, higher spreads might amplify coupons, but they can also increase default risk.

Metric HYG LQD
Effective Duration 3.05 8.14
Convexity -0.15 1.16
OAS 249.79 81.19

Source: iShares

HYG possesses a superior 12-month trailing yield to LQD, meaning its income and capital distributions-to-NAV have dominated. The yields could change, especially as HYG has higher credit risk than LQD. Nonetheless, we think HYG's excess yield is compelling, providing investors with an excess return opportunity.

Vehicle 12-M Trailing Yield
HYG 5.91%
LQD 4.41%

Source: iShares

Lastly, for this section, a view of each ETF's sectoral exposure. Both ETFs have a blend of cyclical and non-cyclical exposure, which we deem favourable in today's environment where tariffs, volatile interest rates, and the development of AI might influence various sectors to different extents. In general, we see no clear relative advantage from either vehicle.

A Cautionary Statement

Economic circumstances can change abruptly and influence defaults. In fact, we've already seen a deterioration of credit risk across U.S. sectors and a surge in speculative grade defaults. We are quite cautious about investing in high-yield bonds, and consider HYG a diamond in the rough more than being optimistic about high-yield vehicles in general. We fear that credit spreads might soon increase given the higher-for-longer interest rate environment, which might influence repayment ability at the lower end of the credit market. However, having said that, we are optimistic about HYG's total return picture for the time being and will dispense our thoughts when circumstances change.

Furthermore, note that both these vehicles are trading at premiums. HYG is at a premium-to-NAV of around 0.23% and LQD 0.06%. These aren't significant premiums, yet they could play a role. Moreover, HYG has an expense ratio of 0.49% and LQD 0.14% – expenses can stack up over a holding period, and shouldn't be neglected.

Final Verdict

Despite being cautious of high-yield bonds at this stage of the credit cycle, we see value in HYG ETF, which focuses on borderline non-IG debt with low effective durations. The vehicle outpaces its investment grade counterpart, LQD, on many fronts, including yield, interest rate sensitivity, and long-term performance.

In essence, we think you can generate similar or excess returns by investing in HYG instead of LQD, unless a tail event occurs.

data.title: Calibre Mining: The Valentine Pour Isn't Baked In Yet
data.description:
Calibre Mining Corp. (OTCQX:CXBMF, TSX:CXB:CA) is a growing mid-sized gold producer with producing assets in both the US and Nicaragua, with a near-term asset coming on-line in Canada later in 2025. The company’s producing assets have put the company on the radar with investors, along with a gold price that is hitting historical highs. This article looks at the new Valentine asset that is coming online and its impact both on the company’s performance and investor’s interest in the company.

Operations

Calibre’s primary operations currently are its Nicaraguan properties which produced 207,220 ounces of gold in 2024, while its Nevada operations added 35,267 ounces on top.

This level of production, as well as a diversity of operations compared to single asset producers, has already put the company on the radar with investors, as we can see from its share price run up over the last couple of years to the point the company now sports a $1.9B market cap.

The key to continued appreciation for the company is the near term commissioning of its Valentine gold mine in Newfoundland, which it purchased for $345m CAD ($249m USD) in acquiring Marathon Gold in 2023. This was a very opportunistic purchase as the feasibility study using a $1,700 gold price (compared to the current price of over $2,900 per ounce) showed an NPV of $648m CAD ($456m USD), well north of the purchase price. Valentine is anticipated to bring an additional 195,000 ounces per annum online at an all in sustaining cost of $1,046 USD, well below the current costs that Calibre reported in Q4 of $1,423. Calibre indicated that this is likely to rise to between $1,500 and $1,600 in 2025 even without the startup of Valentine.

Valentine is planned to pour first gold in Q2 2025 with a steady ramp up to full production by year-end. On its Q4 2024 investor’s call, management indicated that it should produce somewhere between 50 and 100k additional ounces in 2025 from its Valentine operation.

Even with all this production, the company is continuing to invest money in exploration, with $43m being spent in 2024 alone. Calibre has continued to expand its resources at Valentine, with additional pits and further extensions to the acquired resource. They have also found additional resources at its other ongoing operations, as well as its exploration property at Limon.

Catalysts

Management is understandably very excited about the potential for the company to re-rate to a higher multiple, which it feels the commissioning of its Valentine mine will do as it approaches becoming a 500k annual ounce producer. In its presentation, it uses several examples of other companies who introduced new assets that helped the company to re-rate. If we look at the Lassonde curve, we can also see that a lot of value is added at the startup phase as well:

This will understandably bring a massive jump in operative and financial performance, particularly in 2026 when Valentine is near or at its planned performance levels which understandably will bring it much closer to its peers. Posting higher comparatives to the prior year will attract investors, which the production from Valentine will do even during the ramp up stage.

A rising gold price will lift all gold companies including Calibre. Gold has continued to move higher; the reasons behind are anyone’s guess. The debasement of almost all major currencies through increased monetary supply and increasingly unsteady sovereign behaviour backing those currencies could be one cause; central bank buying could be another and there does appear to be some physical shortages at the bullion banks as physical withdrawals seem to be increasing. Whatever the reason, the gold price trend is in a defined uptrend and Calibre will participate as a levered bet on that thesis as well.

Calibre will continue to be exploring on all its properties to advance its reserves, which will eventually lead to an extension of its producing lives (Nicaragua/Canada/US) or further delineation of a future development (Limon). These types of news releases can bring large stock movements with small companies but are ignored by investors in producing companies, so I would not rely on this area as a catalyst for share movements.

Finally, there are still a lot of dislocations in valuations in the gold space. There is still not a lot of capital in the sector and a lot of developers and explorers trade at miniscule market caps compared to their asset; Calibre’s own purchase of Marathon was an example of a larger player taking advantage of a discounted valuation of a premier asset. With the commissioning of Valentine, I would expect to see Calibre start to pound down its debt levels to create expansion capacity; if a corresponding rise in share price comes from a re-rating, it would give Calibre an additional capacity to further diversify its operations.

The flip side to this is that Calibre itself could come in to focus of some of the larger players in the industry as well if its valuation remains at a discount to its peers as its growth profile would help to augment some larger players who have been struggling operationally such as Barrick (GOLD) and Newmont (NEM). These players have not been afraid to spend money to enhance its operations in the past so an acquisition of mid-player like Calibre is not out of the question.

Risks

Execution risk is certainly at play if there is a delay at Valentine of producing gold this late in the process; mining is not an easy business, so it is certainly in the realm of possibility. The fact that management reiterated a Q2 gold pour on its call about Q4 2024 earnings in late February should give some comfort that things are on track. You can also have risks in any operation, from strikes to natural disasters to changes in geology as well; all these could affect any of Calibre’s operations.

The gold price is also a risk; if it were to reverse from current levels, an earnings compression would occur throughout the industry, impacting multiples for all gold producers. There is a wide deviation between Calibre’s all-in sustaining costs (AISC) and the gold price, so they will still produce cash flow, but a falling gold price would certainly be a headwind both financially and in investor’s sentiment.

Valuation

Calibre has included the below comparison in its corporate presentation to other similar sized companies:

With its full-year production closing in on 500k per annum, management is looking at comparing itself to Alamos (AGI) and Lundin (OTCQX:LUGDF) (LUG). Lundin’s AISC will be lower than even a blended rate for Calibre will be including full-year Valentine production, while Alamos will have a similar cost base but at a higher production rate. A 4 or 5x enterprise multiple to Calibre’s current valuation does not seem to be warranted, especially when we look at their pro forma comparatives:

In this Seeking Alpha comparison, we can see that Calibre is discounted on its forward results, and even on its current cash flows, so it looks as though its production expansion has not yet been factored into investors’ valuation of the company.

The Takeaway

With a clear valuation gap to its slightly larger competitors, Calibre gives investors a levered play on a continued rise in gold prices, while also getting a key company catalyst that should elevate its financial performance and its valuation.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Small/Micro Cap Idea investment competition, which runs through February 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Leonardo: Supportive Defense Growth Outlook
data.description:
Following our early January 2025 analysis, "Noisy B787 Challenges, This Is Still A Clear Buy," we were surprised to see a 30% stock price appreciation. We were right to promote Leonardo (OTCPK:FINMF) (OTCPK:FINMY) after a Sell-Off Period, and now the company has reached our target price. Q4 results were broadly in line with our estimates, with no disclosure in 2025. That said, we continue to see significant value trapped within Leonardo's portfolio, and there is also de-risking on the horizon. The company released its preliminary Fiscal Year results on 20 February.

FY Results and Our Positive Take

The Italian defense player reached top-line sales of €17.8 billion (up by 16.2% compared to last year's results). It is impressive that new orders increased to €20.9 billion, representing a book-to-bill ratio higher than 20%. Leonardo's order backlog is not at €44 million, which is equivalent to almost 2.5 years of production. Going down to the P&L analysis, the company's EBITA reached €1.5 billion with a plus 12.9%vs 2023. Q4 sales were 1.5% ahead of Wall Street consensus, with EBITA in line on a comparable basis. We should report that Leonardo's management has changed the definition of EBITA to exclude the restructuring and JV non-recurring costs. By division, we report a solid performance of the helicopter segment and also a beat in the DE&S. There was no detail on 2025 guidance; however, there is a capital market day on 11 March for this. In 2024, Leonardo delivered a FCF of €826 million (plus 26.7% vs 2023)

Why are we still positive?

Our team has covered Leonardo with a buy rating since 2022. We had a record performance with a 300% increase in stock price appreciation. That said, there are still reasons to be positive.

  1. Our team sees a higher defense budget ahead. The JV with Rheinmetall will support Defence growth from 2026 onwards. On Leonardo's domestic market, Italy is still at approximately 1.7% of military expense compared to its 2024 GDP, but NATO pressure is growing to meet ≥ 2% as soon as possible. We also see support in Leonardo Defence Electronics division, with double-digit growth expectations through 2028 vs a guidance of +8%. In December 2024, Leonardo signed a contract to supply the Italian Air Force with up to 24 Eurofighter jets. This deal is worth approximately €7.5 billion. Other public sources report that the Group has signed a big military helicopter order (~28 units) in a Far-East Asian country. Once again, we believe this order may exceed €1.5 billion in value. The company is well positioned to win market share in Italy, and export orders will likely come with multi-year support contracts, improving its FCF visibility;

  2. In our view, the CMD will be the time to reward shareholders. The company now has a solid balance sheet and supportive order visibility. Therefore, we anticipate a buyback for the year. In our estimates, we believe the company will double-digit increase its DPS and return at least 20% of its market capitalization through 2028 (including the buybacks);

  3. Even if, in our estimates, we believe the Aerostructures division is still making losses, we anticipate that the top management team will update investors with a turnaround plan. We expect a deal with an Industrial partner. We believe Aerostructures' deconsolidation business with a potential cash injection has a clear upside, and to us, the downside case is already priced in Leonardo's numbers. Still related to deconsolidation, last week, the Group completed the sale of its underwater business to Fincantieri. Enterprise value was set at €415 million. This is a minor part of the business, and Leonardo will gain EBITA visibility and margin upside ahead;
  4. Leonardo's listed equity portfolio (DRS, Hensoldt & Avio) is worth approximately 25% of its current market cap. Therefore, the company's core business trades at 4x EV/EBIT 2024. Significant value is trapped within its portfolio.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Ahead of the CMD, Leonardo is still a buy. Based on the FY 2024 preliminary results analysis, the company is well-positioned to deliver multi-year support contracts. In numbers, we confirm our 2025 sales to €18.4 billion, but we slightly increase the company EBITA from €1.7 to €2.1 billion. This is due to a better product MIX (Helicopters and Defence Electronics), the Fincantieri deal, cost structure simplification, and lower interest expenses with an additional deleverage of at least €400 million (last year, Leonardo deleveraged by 23% to a Group Net Debt of €1.8 billion). Applying a 10x EBITA multiple, our standalone equity value reached €21 billion. However, we should consider the company's primary holding equity stakes, which are 1) Leonardo DRS for €3.63 billion, 2) Hensoldt AG for €810 million, and 3) Avio for a total consideration of €100 million. Therefore, our enterprise value is approximately €25.5 billion, and considering the current debt, our equity value stands at €23.7 billion. This implies an 18% stock price appreciation.

Risks

We have extensive coverage of Leonardo, so we report the following downside risks: 1) Lower GDP military expenses with no more conflict in Ukraine and other parts of the World, 2) continued disruption in lower B787 deliveries and no turnaround plan (which we believe it is unlikely), 3) persistent inflation with COGS price changes, 4) wage inflation, 5) FX fluctuations given the contract time duration, 6) supply chain constraints, 7) non-ideal corporate governance (the Italian government's equity stake 30.2%), and 8) Leonardo derives a quarter of its expected earnings from investments in Joint Ventures. Potential lower dividend distribution might impact the company's debt position.

Conclusion

New order intake thanks to a higher military budget, significant value trapped within Leonardo's portfolio, and upside from shareholders' rewards will support another group re-rating. Our buy is then confirmed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: Post-Earnings Dip Is An Excellent Entry Point As Revenue Growth Excels
data.description:
So far in the Q4 earnings season, a lot of momentum-driven growth stocks have seen their rallies knocked off course, as investors brought sky-high expectations to bear against rich valuations. For patient and long term-oriented investors, however, some of the post-earnings declines represent great re-entry points into incredible growth stocks that were previously running a bit too expensive to buy.

Sprouts Farmers Market (NASDAQ:SFM), in my view, is one of these names. The grocery chain, known for its unique selection of farm-fresh products, shed ~15% after reporting a Q4 earnings beat, despite an acceleration in revenue growth. As can perhaps be expected, however, investors reacted quite sharply to the company's implication of deceleration in its FY25 outlook. To me, however, the company is setting itself up for a good beat-and-raise cadence this year, and this temporary lull in a stock that has risen more than 2x over the past year is a well-timed buying opportunity.

I last wrote a bullish article on Sprouts in November, when the stock was still trading in the mid-$130s. I've gained a decent amount on my position since then, but amid the sharp post-earnings drop, I think investors who haven't. Yet gotten exposure to this fantastic growth stock, have another chance to buy Sprouts at a decent (though certainly not cheap) valuation. As such, I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

In my view, there are a number of positives anchoring the long-term bull case for Sprouts. The principal tailwind for the company is the growing and secular shift toward healthy living. More and more consumers are prioritizing clean diets. Sprouts prides itself on unique product curation; the items it carries tend to be different from what can be found in typical grocery stores. 70% of its products are what the company calls “attribute driven,” such as organic, paleo, keto, vegan, gluten-free. The majority are also locally sourced, allowing for differentiation between locations.

Note that these tailwinds aren't just anecdotal. We can see evidence for Sprouts' growing momentum in its own acceleration of same-store sales, helping to cement the thesis that healthy-eating trends are helping to drive growth for Sprouts.

It's worth noting as well that Sprouts' overall footprint and penetration in the grocery space is relatively low. Market estimates peg annual U.S. grocery sales at north of $700 billion, which means that Sprouts' ~$8 billion in annual revenue is barely at a 1% share of this overall market. The company is currently present in only 24 states, and is planning for 10%+ annual unit expansion, with a focus on high-density markets on the West Coast, Northeast, and Texas.

All in all, I recommend staying focused on the long-term growth opportunity here and using the near-term dip as a buying moment.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Sprouts' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Sprouts' revenue grew 18% y/y to $2.00 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of just $1.96 billion (+16% y/y) by a two-point margin, while importantly also accelerating four points relative to 14% y/y growth in Q3.

The company opened 12 net-new stores in the quarter, which contributed to the revenue acceleration. But it's also important to recognize as well that same store sales comps also accelerated (representing sales growth from locations that already existed in the prior-year compare period), clocking in at 11.5% comps growth in Q4 (versus 8.4% in Q3 and 6.7% in Q2).

Again, it's worth emphasizing here that Sprouts' ability to accelerate to double-digit same-store sales growth in a retail category that is essentially a staple with flattish growth trends is a testament to how Sprouts' product-led, healthy-distinguished brand orientation is helping the company to accrue meaningful market share.

We note that the company's roadmap for expansion remains aggressive. With the company now at 440 stores across 24 states in the U.S. (the latest state launch was Wyoming), the company also has 110 planned developments in the pipeline, and is planning to open at least 35 net-new stores in FY25 (we note this is a bit shy of the company's long-term target of 10%+ unit growth, but there's a good chance that development timelines may compress and lead to more net openings in FY25). Per CFO Curtis Valentine's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

This strong comp performance was broad based and balanced across channels, geographies, baskets and traffic. October was the strongest comp month with both of the other months in the quarter also showing double digit comp growth [...]

We ended the year with 440 stores across 24 states. For the years ahead, we have over 110 approved new stores and nearly 70 executed leases in the pipeline. A strong and healthy balance sheet has underpinned our financial performance."

On the profitability front, we note that Sprouts' pro forma gross margins rose by an impressive 160bps y/y to 38.1%. The company notes that improved inventory management systems have helped to reduce shrink, while the company's expansion of its store-owned Sprouts Brand has also expanded to 23% of the company's overall sales.

Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA in the fourth quarter also jumped 38% y/y to $142.7 million, representing a 7.1% margin, 100bps better than 6.1% in the year-ago quarter. The company's pro forma EPS of $0.79 in the quarter also beat Street expectations of $0.73 with 8% upside.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

After Sprouts' post-earnings drop to the low $140s, Sprouts trades at a market cap of $14.33 billion. After we net off the $265.2 million of cash and minor $7.2 million of debt on Sprouts' latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $14.07 billion.

Meanwhile, we note the company is guiding to 10.5-12.5% y/y revenue growth in FY25 (on the back of 4.5-6.5% comparable sales growth - the implied deceleration from 11.5% comp growth in Q4 is likely the biggest driver behind the stock's fall), and $590-$610 million of adjusted EBIT (+19% y/y at the midpoint), and $4.52-$4.68 in pro forma EPS (+23% y/y at the midpoint).

This puts Sprouts' valuation multiples at:

  • 23.4x EV/FY25 adjusted EBIT
  • 31x FY25 P/E

To me, a valuation multiple that tends to run a bit hot is one of the biggest risks that Sprouts faces - but at the same time, we note that the company's high-20s earnings growth, with the potential for faster EPS jumps if the company's sales comps don't decelerate as much as expected or if gross margins continue improving, affords Sprouts at least some premium.

Valuation risk is also joined by several fundamental risks. Inflation is the biggest one: the soaring price of eggs has become one of the biggest domestic headlines of 2025, and continued pressure on consumer budgets may push consumers to trade down from more expensive Sprouts products to cheaper grocery store wares.

That being said, I still see plenty of white space for Sprouts to grow beyond its current ~1% share of the U.S. grocery market, especially with several quarters of accelerating comparable sales under its belt. I'm willing to pay Sprouts' modest premium to hold this stock for the long haul, and the post-earnings dip is a good moment for investors who don't have this stock yet to jump in.

data.title: The Trade Desk Crashes: Here's The Price I'll Buy (Again!).
data.description:
What happened to The Trade Desk stock?

Wow! Just when it looks like high-flying stocks will continue defying reasonable valuations, the market delivers a bruising lesson. The Trade Desk released what the market deemed disappointing results. Revenue came in at $741 million, $19 million below expectations. Before Q4, The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) hadn't posted an earnings miss in 33 straight quarters.

The results weren't awful, but it wasn't good enough when a stock trades for more than 20 times sales. As you can see below, the stock plummeted.

It is nearly 50% off its recent highs. I've been bullish about The Trade Desk, especially in 2022 and early 2023, when I built a position from $42 to $68 per share. The majority of my shares were called away after selling $105 calls, sold more at $130, and have had just a token position since.

It's looking like time to get back in soon.

How bad were Q4 earnings?

It's not that Q4 was abysmal, and the full year of 2024 was quite good. As shown below, sales grew 26% for the year, an increase over the 23% in 2023.

However, guidance for Q1 2025 is for just 17% growth - a major backward step.

Founder and CEO Jeff Green was contrite on the earnings call:

While we’re proud of these milestones, I want to acknowledge up front that for the first time in 33 quarters as a public company, we fell short of our own expectations. During COVID, we revised our expectations once along with the rest of the markets, but for the first time in 8 years, we missed the expectations we set, and it was our fault.

....we did the largest reorganization in company history in December. While we often make structural changes at the end of the year to improve our business, this was bigger than usual.

This sounds ominous. Green went on to lay out a four-stage plan of action.

  1. The company clarified employees' roles and streamlined sales teams.
  2. Renewed focus on internal effectiveness and scalability.
  3. Dedicated more resources to brand relationships and joint business plans (this part of the business is growing much quicker than other areas).
  4. Shrunk product development teams to become more nimble.

Reading between the lines: It sounds like the company became stagnant, had too many cooks in the kitchen, the salesforce wasn't performing as well as expected, and new products were too slow to come to the market.

This isn't good news; however, recognizing the problems and formulating a detailed action plan is critical. And that is what Green did. COVID-19 forced Airbnb to do something similar in 2020, and the results have been terrific. Here are Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky's comments on the shift:

…on headcount, something that's really interesting happened. So obviously, in 2020, we had to make some really difficult decisions, and we became a much smaller and more focused company. And the obvious result of that is that we got more efficient and more profitable. But there was a less obvious result. What ended up happening is we had fewer people in meetings, and people can move a lot faster. And we concentrate all of our very best people and put them on only a few problems.

The streamlining was very successful. Jeff Green is a terrific CEO, so my confidence is high that the changes will work well over the long haul.

Is The Trade Desk a buy now?

The Trade Desk is well positioned in a growing industry. Programmatic advertising and streaming television will continue to increase. Advertisers love data, feedback, and targeted placement, which is The Trade Desk's wheelhouse. According to Perplexity, the average American home subscribes to 4.5 streaming services in 2025, up from 3.9 last year.

Live sports is a tailwind for streaming as well. The latest development is Netflix (NFLX) reportedly preparing a bid for a full slate of Sunday NFL football game rights. The exodus of sports from cable will continue since the major networks don't have as deep of pockets as the Netflix and Amazons (AMZN) of the world.

The company's finances are in excellent shape. Cash and investments increased from $1.4 billion as of Q4 2023 to $1.9 billion at the end of 2024. Receivables also increased from $2.9 billion to $3.3 billion, and the company is long-term debt-free.

GAAP operating income for 2024 increased to $430 million in 2024 compared to $200 million the prior year. The operating margin grew to 17% from 10%, proving the company has operating leverage.

Finally (finally!), The Trade Desk's stock valuation is attractive again. We are nearly back to the low levels at the beginning of 2023.

On sales:

And on cash flow:

The stock's valuation has nearly retraced all the gains over the last two years.

I am bullish on The Trade Desk and have been waiting for an opportunity to buy shares again. This looks like a good time to begin rebuilding a position. The changes will take time to succeed, so the stock may drift for a while. However, the company released Q1 guidance on February 12th, nearly halfway through the quarter. You can bet they are confident they will beat those numbers.

data.title: S&P 500: It's Happening Again. Week Starting 24th February (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
It was another red Friday for the S&P 500 (SPY), the fifth in a row. It makes you wonder what news could come out over the weekend and the large Monday drops on 27th January (Deep Seek) and the 3rd February (the trade war) seem to have made investors risk averse into the end of the week.

Last weekend's article pointed out the bullish technicals and the potential for a break-out. This looked to be playing out as Wednesday's session closed at a new all-time high of 6144, but things quickly soured and the break of 6100 warned all was not right (see last week's final sentence).

This week's article looks at why Friday's drop could be another sharp but short-lived move and explores how something more bearish could develop.

S&P 500 Monthly

There is only one week to go until February's close and it is fulfilling the stat that February is traditionally one of the weaker months of the year. Last week's rally made a new all-time high, but by Friday the S&P500 was already trading below January's close. A potentially bearish bar (a doji) would form with a close near or below the February open of 5969.

The S&P500 has struggled to break the measured move and Fibonacci extension highlighted in these articles since September. 6118 is where the bull market starting at the 2022 low is equal in length to the 2020-2022 bull market 6145 is the 200% extension of the 2022 bear market.

Support on the monthly chart is obvious at the January 5773 low, but it is also worth noting the December close of 5881 where the 2025 annual bar would shift from green to red. 5669-74 at the July high and the October bottom is a major support level below that.

An upside DeMARK exhaustion count is on bar 5 (of 9) in February. This means there can be no signal until at least May.

S&P 500 Weekly

This week's bar was bearish with a failed new high and weak close at the lows. It's the kind of action that can lead to tops/large corrections and if the monthly chart also develops a reversal, we should be extra cautious. Note that none of the previous five weeks had a bearish pattern which made it easier to buy the dips on DeepSeek, tariffs, CPI, etc...

Resistance is at 6100 and 6147. The red channel high will be above 6300 next week, so it is too distant to be relevant.

Initial support is the weekly low of 6003. The weekly 20sma is worth noting around 5960 as it caught several bottoms, but it has also been cut through several times so it should be in confluence with other factors to be trustworthy.

An upside DeMARK exhaustion count was aborted this week by the drop. Failure to reach completion is a feature of choppy markets. A new downside count will be on bar 2 (of 9) next week.

S&P 500 Daily

Wednesday's session could only be described as quietly bullish. Thursday's session was a warning but could still be regarded as neutral. Friday's drop was therefore slightly surprising, although by now we should all be on guard going into the weekend. Good supports were broken and the weak close at the 50sma suggests there will be follow-through next week.

Initial resistance is around 6050 at the high volume area, then 6084-100.

6003-6008 is initial support at the previous low and the 50sma but looks vulnerable. 5962 is a previous reversal point and lines up with the 20sma. Below that, the 5923 low and the blue channel at 5945 are major support.

A downside DeMARK exhaustion count will be on bar 2 (of 9) on Monday. That means no signal can be completed next week. Indeed, no signal has been completed since 4th December as trends keep reversing.

Drivers/Events

Data was on the quiet side last week and the sell-off didn't have one principal driver; rather it seemed a combination of recent red flags. Market moves on Friday support the view that the fears revolve around a slower economy. Oil dropped sharply and bonds were higher. Walmart (WMT) earnings and lower-than-expected consumer sentiment were just the cherry on the top.

Recent failed break-outs underline the view that Mag 7 stocks must participate for any sustained rally to unfold. Nvidia's (NVDA) earnings next Wednesday may be the deciding factor on whether this important group of stocks will lead again or continue to stagnate. Guidance will be watched closely in light of DeepSeek.

Prelim GDP and unemployment claims will be released on Thursday. The data highlight of the week is on Friday with the release of the PCE Index. This is expected to rise to 0.3%, which is too high but not a level to cause panic. A few lower prints in the following months combined with weaker data would still open the door for Fed cuts in May/June.

One last thing to monitor is USDJPY. With yields rising in Japan and falling in the US, is the carry trade unwinding and putting additional pressure on stocks?

Probable Moves Next Week(s)

The S&P500 has done it again: another failed break-out and another Friday plunge. This time it has created a weekly reversal and the monthly chart could also develop a reversal pattern if the February bar closes below 5969 this coming Friday. It makes buying the dip riskier than in previous weeks when the technicals were still clearly bullish, but there is not enough evidence yet to say this is different than other sharp drops in recent months.

Friday's action suggests there will be more downside on Monday or Tuesday. This could come immediately after some negative news out over the weekend, or following a brief bounce if bad news fails to materialize. 6050 may act as initial resistance. A break of 6000 should target 5945-62 where this leg down could end and lead to another full recovery to 6100 and even new highs.

Bigger picture, the sluggish action over the last 3 months should eventually lead to a larger correction to reset positioning and sentiment. 5669 is a reasonable initial target in this phase. Closing next week below 5969 would suggest it is already underway.
